text: This is a seventy seven year old patient who has been diagnosed with achalasia. The video will illustrate a robotic helleromyotomy and dwarf endoplication.
The surgeon uses a harmonic scalpel to take down the gastropag limit, followed by addressing the right crus.
The surgeon dissects the esophagus, separating the attachments of the stomach from the left crux using surgical instruments.
The surgeon performs mediastinal dissection of the esophagus using a harmonic scalpel and blunt dissection techniques, carefully separating the esophagus from surrounding mediastinal tissues.
The surgeon dissects and encircles the vagus nerve, fully mobilizing it away from the esophagus to prepare for the myotomy.
The surgeon initiates the myotomy using electrocautery, carefully separating the longitudinal muscles of the esophagus.
The circular muscle layers of the esophagus are visible, showcasing the distinct muscular structure during the dissection process.
The surgeon cauterizes the tissue attachments to expose the submucosa of the esophagus, revealing the underlying layers necessary for further dissection.
The robotic system is used to grasp both sides of the esophageal myotomy with precision, facilitating the delivery of the esophagus into the abdomen and allowing access to the upper esophagus.
The surgeon uses a hook to complete the myotomy, carefully separating the remaining esophageal muscle fibers.
The surgeon carefully touches tissues adjacent to the esophagus with the hook to cool it down, preventing thermal injury to the exposed submucosa during the myotomy.
The surgeon uses robotic retraction arms to pull the esophagus down into the abdomen, folding the muscle layers outward like an open book, revealing the circular muscle layers of the esophagus.

The surgeon extends the myotomy up to the level of the inferior pulmonary veins and continues it down onto the gastroesophageal junction, reaching approximately two to three centimeters onto the stomach.
The vagus nerve is visible traversing the area of the completed myotomy on the esophagus.

The camera provides a view of the completed myotomy, extending from the mid esophagus down onto the stomach, spanning over six centimeters above the gastroesophageal junction and two centimeters below it.
The surgeon divides the short gastric vessels to mobilize the fundus of the stomach, preparing for the door fundoplication portion of the procedure.
The surgeon begins the repair by suturing the left crus of the diaphragm to the left side of the myotomy muscle, securing the anatomical structures in place.
The surgeon uses a two-oethabon suture, visible in the surgical field, likely for securing anatomical structures involved in the fundoplication procedure.
The surgeon tacks the angle of His to the diaphragmatic crura, securing the anatomical position during the Nissen fundoplication procedure.
The surgeon positions the fundus of the stomach over the esophagus, effectively covering the myotomy site.
The surgeon places a stitch into the stomach, extending it to the right side of the myotomy muscle, and then secures it to the right crus of the diaphragm.
The surgeon places another stitch from the fundus of the stomach to the right side of the myotomy muscle, then secures it to the right cruce of the diaphragm.
The surgeon uses sutures to tack the stomach to the anterior defect, effectively closing the defect and the hiatus.
This patient did well postoperatively. Post-op esophagram demonstrated no leak. The patient was sent home on a full liquid diet on postoperative day number one.
